title: Phrasal Verbs
description: Essential English phrasal verbs used in everyday and professional contexts

cards.meaning: To stop working; or to analyze into smaller parts
cards.example: My car broke down on the highway. / Break down the problem into smaller steps.

cards.meaning: To mention a topic; or to raise a child
cards.example: She brought up an interesting point in the meeting.

cards.meaning: To cancel something
cards.example: The game was called off due to bad weather.

cards.meaning: To perform or complete a task
cards.example: We need to carry out more tests before launching.

cards.meaning: To reach the same level as others; to learn what you missed
cards.example: I need to catch up on my reading after being sick.

cards.meaning: To register at a hotel or airport
cards.example: We checked in to the hotel at 3 PM.

cards.meaning: To leave a hotel; to look at something
cards.example: We checked out at noon. / Check out this amazing view!

cards.meaning: To find or meet by chance
cards.example: I came across an interesting article online.

cards.meaning: To think of or produce an idea or plan
cards.example: She came up with a brilliant solution.

cards.meaning: To reduce the amount of something
cards.example: I need to cut down on sugar in my diet.

cards.meaning: To handle or manage a situation
cards.example: How do you deal with stress at work?

cards.meaning: To deliver someone or something; to decrease
cards.example: Can you drop me off at the station?

cards.meaning: To finally reach a state or place
cards.example: We ended up eating at a street food stall.

cards.meaning: To not keep up with others or a schedule
cards.example: He fell behind on his coursework after the illness.

cards.meaning: To understand or find the solution to something
cards.example: I couldn't figure out how to use the software.

cards.meaning: To complete a form; to substitute for someone
cards.example: Please fill in the registration form.

cards.meaning: To discover information
cards.example: Did you find out what caused the error?

cards.meaning: To have a friendly relationship with someone
cards.example: She gets along well with all her colleagues.

cards.meaning: To escape blame or punishment
cards.example: He got away with submitting the report late.

cards.meaning: To contact someone later; to return to a topic
cards.example: I'll get back to you on that by Friday.

cards.meaning: To recover from illness or difficulty
cards.example: It took months to get over the breakup.

cards.meaning: To stop trying or doing something
cards.example: Never give up on your dreams.

cards.meaning: To proceed or continue with something
cards.example: Please go ahead with the presentation.

cards.meaning: To experience something difficult; to examine carefully
cards.example: She went through a difficult period last year.

cards.meaning: To submit work to someone in authority
cards.example: Please hand in your assignment by Friday.

cards.meaning: To wait; to grip tightly
cards.example: Hold on, I need to check this.

cards.meaning: To stay at the same level or pace as someone
cards.example: It's hard to keep up with all the changes in technology.

cards.meaning: To start an event or process
cards.example: The conference kicks off tomorrow morning.

cards.meaning: To dismiss workers; to stop doing something
cards.example: The company laid off 200 employees.

cards.meaning: To not include something
cards.example: Don't leave out any important details.

cards.meaning: To disappoint someone
cards.example: He let down the whole team by missing the deadline.

cards.meaning: To take care of someone or something
cards.example: Can you look after my dog while I'm away?

cards.meaning: To feel excited about something in the future
cards.example: I'm looking forward to the holiday.

cards.meaning: To investigate or examine something
cards.example: We'll look into the complaint immediately.

cards.meaning: To search for information; to improve
cards.example: Look up the word in a dictionary.

cards.meaning: To invent a story; to reconcile after a disagreement
cards.example: Stop making up excuses. / They made up after the argument.

cards.meaning: To continue to the next thing; to stop dwelling on the past
cards.example: It's time to move on and look for a new job.

cards.meaning: To give information or an object to someone else
cards.example: Please pass on this message to the manager.

cards.meaning: To collect; to learn something quickly
cards.example: Can you pick up the kids from school?

cards.meaning: To direct attention to something
cards.example: She pointed out a mistake in my report.

cards.meaning: To postpone; to discourage
cards.example: Don't put off things you can do today.

cards.meaning: To tolerate something unpleasant
cards.example: I can't put up with noise while working.

cards.meaning: To meet someone unexpectedly; to encounter a problem
cards.example: I ran into an old friend at the market.

cards.meaning: To use up all of something
cards.example: We ran out of ink during the presentation.

cards.meaning: To establish or arrange something
cards.example: She set up a new business last year.

cards.meaning: To arrive or appear somewhere
cards.example: He didn't show up to the meeting.

cards.meaning: To reduce speed; to become less busy or active
cards.example: Please slow down when driving in the rain.

cards.meaning: To be noticeably different or better
cards.example: Her presentation really stood out from the rest.

cards.meaning: To accept responsibility for a task
cards.example: She took on extra work to help the team.

cards.meaning: To assume control of something
cards.example: A new CEO took over the company in March.

cards.meaning: To reject or refuse; to reduce volume/heat
cards.example: He turned down the job offer.

cards.meaning: To arrive; to increase volume
cards.example: She turned up an hour late to the event.

cards.meaning: To finish or conclude something
cards.example: Let's wrap up this meeting by 5 PM.

cards.meaning: To cause something to happen
cards.example: The new policy brought about significant changes.

cards.meaning: To require or demand something
cards.example: The situation calls for immediate action.

cards.meaning: To reduce spending or consumption
cards.example: The company cut back on travel expenses.

cards.meaning: To use experience or resources for help
cards.example: She drew on her years of experience.

cards.meaning: To accept and deal with a difficult situation
cards.example: You need to face up to your mistakes.

cards.meaning: To fail to happen as planned
cards.example: The deal fell through at the last minute.

cards.meaning: To pursue or check on something further
cards.example: I'll follow up on this with a call tomorrow.

cards.meaning: To prepare for something
cards.example: The team is gearing up for the product launch.

cards.meaning: To restrain from doing something
cards.example: Don't hold back your creative ideas.

cards.meaning: To resolve small problems or difficulties
cards.example: We need to iron out the final details of the contract.

cards.meaning: To join a conversation or activity suddenly
cards.example: Please jump in if you have any questions.

cards.meaning: To continue doing something
cards.example: Keep on practicing and you'll improve.

cards.meaning: To stop increasing or decreasing and become stable
cards.example: Sales levelled off after the initial spike.

cards.meaning: To reduce options to a smaller number
cards.example: We've narrowed down the candidates to three.

cards.meaning: To choose not to participate in something
cards.example: You can opt out of the newsletter at any time.

cards.meaning: To gradually stop using or doing something
cards.example: The company is phasing out paper contracts.

cards.meaning: To introduce something to the public or a group
cards.example: The new software update will roll out next week.

cards.meaning: To increase the size or scope of something
cards.example: We plan to scale up production next quarter.

cards.meaning: To resolve a problem or organize things
cards.example: Let me sort out the scheduling conflict.

cards.meaning: To increase effort; to take on more responsibility
cards.example: He stepped up when the project manager was away.

cards.meaning: To give a summary of the main points
cards.example: To sum up, the key findings are these three.

cards.meaning: To consider something carefully before deciding
cards.example: Think through all the options before committing.

cards.meaning: To calculate; to exercise; to resolve a problem
cards.example: We need to work out the total cost. / She works out every morning.

cards.meaning: To support someone; to make a copy of data
cards.example: Can you back up your claims with evidence?

cards.meaning: To explode; to suddenly become very angry
cards.example: He blew up when he heard the news.

cards.meaning: To enter a place by force; to start a new career field
cards.example: It is hard to break into the film industry.

cards.meaning: To review or improve skills that you have not used recently
cards.example: I need to brush up on my French before the trip.

cards.meaning: To become exhausted from too much work
cards.example: Many teachers burn out after just a few years.

cards.meaning: To contribute money or effort to a group cause
cards.example: Everyone chipped in to buy the farewell gift.

cards.meaning: To take strict action to stop something
cards.example: The government clamped down on tax evasion.

cards.meaning: To rely or depend on someone
cards.example: You can always count on her to help.

cards.meaning: To take severe measures against something
cards.example: Police are cracking down on speeding in school zones.

cards.meaning: To continue for longer than expected
cards.example: The meeting dragged on for three hours.

cards.meaning: To break into pieces; to fail completely
cards.example: The plan fell apart when the funding was cut.

cards.meaning: To give something to someone officially
cards.example: She handed over the documents to the lawyer.

cards.meaning: To spend time relaxing with others
cards.example: We usually hang out at the coffee shop on weekends.

cards.meaning: To stand in a row; to organize or arrange
cards.example: We lined up several speakers for the conference.

cards.meaning: To meet expectations or standards
cards.example: The product did not live up to the hype.

cards.meaning: To reduce the price of something
cards.example: All winter coats have been marked down by thirty percent.

cards.meaning: To succeed in doing something difficult
cards.example: She pulled off a brilliant presentation despite the short notice.

cards.meaning: To eliminate as a possibility
cards.example: We cannot rule out the possibility of delays.

cards.meaning: To register or enroll in something
cards.example: I signed up for an online cooking course.

cards.meaning: To talk louder; to express your opinion
cards.example: If you disagree, please speak up.

cards.meaning: To gradually relax after a period of activity
cards.example: She likes to wind down with a book before bed.

cards.meaning: To focus attention on a specific thing
cards.example: The investigation zeroed in on the main suspect.

cards.meaning: To add more to something to make it full again
cards.example: Can I top up your glass with more water?

cards.meaning: To consider something carefully before making a decision
cards.example: We need to weigh up the pros and cons before deciding.
